# Load necessary libraries
library(readxl)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
library(tidyr)

Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

## # A tibble: 4 × 14
##   `HE provider`                    01 Medicine and dent…¹ 02 Subjects allied t…²
##   <chr>                                             <dbl>                  <dbl>
## 1 Newcastle University                               3000                   2430
## 2 University of Northumbria at Ne…                     10                   3940
## 3 University Centre Quayside Limi…                      0                      0
## 4 Total                                              3010                   6370
## # ℹ abbreviated names: ¹​`01 Medicine and dentistry`,
## #   ²​`02 Subjects allied to medicine`
## # ℹ 11 more variables: `03 Biological and sport sciences` <dbl>,
## #   `04 Psychology` <dbl>, `05 Veterinary sciences` <dbl>,
## #   `06 Agriculture, food and related studies` <dbl>,
## #   `07 Physical sciences` <dbl>, `09 Mathematical sciences` <dbl>,
## #   `10 Engineering and technology` <dbl>, `11 Computing` <dbl>, …
Newcastle = read_excel("subject_of_study_2(science).xlsx", sheet = 'Newcastle')
head(Newcastle)
## # A tibble: 4 × 14
##   `HE provider`                    01 Medicine and dent…¹ 02 Subjects allied t…²
##   <chr>                                             <dbl>                  <dbl>
## 1 Newcastle University                               3000                   2430
## 2 University of Northumbria at Ne…                     10                   3940
## 3 University Centre Quayside Limi…                      0                      0
## 4 Total                                              3010                   6370
## # ℹ abbreviated names: ¹​`01 Medicine and dentistry`,
## #   ²​`02 Subjects allied to medicine`
## # ℹ 11 more variables: `03 Biological and sport sciences` <dbl>,
## #   `04 Psychology` <dbl>, `05 Veterinary sciences` <dbl>,
## #   `06 Agriculture, food and related studies` <dbl>,
## #   `07 Physical sciences` <dbl>, `09 Mathematical sciences` <dbl>,
## #   `10 Engineering and technology` <dbl>, `11 Computing` <dbl>, …
Liverpool = read_excel("subject_of_study_2(science).xlsx", sheet = 'Liverpool')
head(Liverpool)
## # A tibble: 6 × 14
##   `HE provider`                    01 Medicine and dent…¹ 02 Subjects allied t…²
##   <chr>                                             <dbl>                  <dbl>
## 1 Liverpool Hope University                             0                     65
## 2 Liverpool John Moores University                      0                   5555
## 3 The Liverpool Institute for Per…                      0                      0
## 4 The University of Liverpool                        2455                   2305
## 5 Liverpool School of Tropical Me…                    250                     10
## 6 Total                                              2705                   7935
## # ℹ abbreviated names: ¹​`01 Medicine and dentistry`,
## #   ²​`02 Subjects allied to medicine`
## # ℹ 11 more variables: `03 Biological and sport sciences` <dbl>,
## #   `04 Psychology` <dbl>, `05 Veterinary sciences` <dbl>,
## #   `06 Agriculture, food and related studies` <dbl>,
## #   `07 Physical sciences` <dbl>, `09 Mathematical sciences` <dbl>,
## #   `10 Engineering and technology` <dbl>, `11 Computing` <dbl>, …
middlesbrough = read_excel("subject_of_study_2(science).xlsx", sheet = 'middlesbrough')
head(middlesbrough)
## # A tibble: 1 × 14
##   `HE provider`       `01 Medicine and dentistry` 02 Subjects allied to medici…¹
##   <chr>                                     <dbl>                          <dbl>
## 1 Teesside University                         140                           7150
## # ℹ abbreviated name: ¹​`02 Subjects allied to medicine`
## # ℹ 11 more variables: `03 Biological and sport sciences` <dbl>,
## #   `04 Psychology` <dbl>, `05 Veterinary sciences` <dbl>,
## #   `06 Agriculture, food and related studies` <dbl>,
## #   `07 Physical sciences` <dbl>, `09 Mathematical sciences` <dbl>,
## #   `10 Engineering and technology` <dbl>, `11 Computing` <dbl>,
## #   `13 Architecture, building and planning` <dbl>, …
dataset_nonscience = read_excel("subject_of_study(nonscience).xlsx")
head(dataset_nonscience)
## # A tibble: 4 × 12
##   `HE provider`             `15 Social sciences` `16 Law` 17 Business and mana…¹
##   <chr>                                    <dbl>    <dbl>                  <dbl>
## 1 Newcastle University                      1805      800                   3260
## 2 University of Northumbri…                 3495     1720                   7790
## 3 University Centre Quaysi…                    0        0                    155
## 4 Total                                     5300     2520                  11205
## # ℹ abbreviated name: ¹​`17 Business and management`
## # ℹ 8 more variables: `19 Language and area studies` <dbl>,
## #   `20 Historical, philosophical and religious studies` <dbl>,
## #   `22 Education and teaching` <dbl>, `23 Combined and general studies` <dbl>,
## #   `24 Media, journalism and communications` <dbl>,
## #   `25 Design, and creative and performing arts` <dbl>,
## #   `26 Geography, earth and environmental studies (social sciences)` <dbl>, …
# Reshape the science dataset into long format
science_long <- dataset %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -`HE provider`,  # Exclude the 'HE provider' column
    names_to = "Subject",   # Column names will be subjects
    values_to = "Count"     # The cell values will be the counts
  ) %>%
  filter(`HE provider` != "Total") %>%
  mutate(Category = "Science")  # Label this dataset as "Science"

# Reshape the non-science dataset into long format
non_science_long <- dataset_nonscience %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -`HE provider`,  # Exclude the 'HE provider' column
    names_to = "Subject",   # Column names will be subjects
    values_to = "Count"     # The cell values will be the counts
  ) %>%
  filter(`HE provider` != "Total") %>%
  mutate(Category = "Non-Science")  # Label this dataset as "Non-Science"

# Combine the two datasets into one
combined_data <- bind_rows(science_long, non_science_long)

# Summing the counts for Science and Non-Science for each HE provider (university)
totals <- combined_data %>%
  group_by(`HE provider`, Category) %>%
  summarise(Total_Count = sum(Count), .groups = "drop")

# Create a bar plot showing total science vs non-science counts for each university
ggplot(totals, aes(x = `HE provider`, y = Total_Count, fill = Category)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge") +  # Create a grouped bar plot
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Total Number of Science vs Non-Science Subjects in Newcastle Universities",
    x = "University",
    y = "Total Count"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Science" = "skyblue", "Non-Science" = "orange")) +  # Customize colors
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))  # Rotate x-axis labels for better readability

gender = read_excel("study_of_gender.xlsx")
head(gender)
## # A tibble: 4 × 5
##   `HE provider`                          Female  Male Unknown  Total
##   <chr>                                   <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl>
## 1 Newcastle University                    14930 12835      85  27850
## 2 University of Northumbria at Newcastle  17625 14920      20  32565
## 3 University Centre Quayside Limited         65    95       0    155
## 4 Total                                   72075 57085     185 129345
# Load necessary libraries
library(readxl)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Load the data
gender_data <- read_excel("study_of_gender.xlsx", sheet = "gender") %>%
  rename_all(trimws) # Remove extra spaces from column names

# Function to create a pie chart for a university
create_pie_chart <- function(data, university_name) {
  # Filter data for the specific university
  university_data <- dplyr::filter(data, `HE provider` == university_name)
  
  if (nrow(university_data) == 0) {
    message(paste("No data found for", university_name))
    return(NULL)
  }
  
  # Prepare data for the pie chart
  gender_counts <- university_data %>%
    select(Female, Male, Unknown) %>%
    as.numeric()
  gender_labels <- c("Female", "Male", "Unknown")
  
  if (sum(gender_counts, na.rm = TRUE) == 0) {
    message(paste("Skipping", university_name, "- all counts are zero or missing"))
    return(NULL)
  }
  
  # Create pie chart
  pie_data <- data.frame(
    Gender = gender_labels,
    Count = gender_counts
  )
  
  ggplot(pie_data, aes(x = "", y = Count, fill = Gender)) +
    geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 1) +
    coord_polar("y") +
    labs(
      title = paste("Gender Distribution -", university_name),
      x = NULL,
      y = NULL
    ) +
    geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(Count / sum(Count) * 100, 1), "%")), 
              position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5)) +
    theme_void() +
    scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2")
}

# Generate pie charts for Newcastle universities
create_pie_chart(gender_data, "Newcastle University")

create_pie_chart(gender_data, "University of Northumbria at Newcastle")

create_pie_chart(gender_data, "University Centre Quayside Limited")

# Filter data for specific HE providers
gender_filtered <- gender %>%
  dplyr::filter(`HE provider` %in% c("Newcastle University", 
                                     "University of Northumbria at Newcastle", 
                                     "University Centre Quayside Limited")) %>%
  select(-Total) %>%  # Exclude 'Total' column
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Male, Female, Unknown), 
               names_to = "Category", 
               values_to = "Count")

# Summarize data to calculate total count for each gender category
gender_summary <- gender_filtered %>%
  group_by(Category) %>%
  summarise(Total_Count = sum(Count))

# Create Pie Chart
ggplot(gender_summary, aes(x = "", y = Total_Count, fill = Category)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 1) +
  coord_polar("y", start = 0) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Gender Distribution in Universities of Newcastle") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2")

Pie Charts for Individual Universities in Newcastle

# Load necessary libraries
library(dplyr)

# Filter Newcastle universities
newcastle_data <- gender_data %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("Newcastle|Quayside", `HE provider`, ignore.case = TRUE))

# Generate pie charts for each university
for (i in seq_len(nrow(newcastle_data))) {
  university <- newcastle_data[i, ]
  gender_counts <- c(university$Female, university$Male, university$Unknown)
  labels <- c("Female", "Male", "Unknown")
  
  # Create pie chart
  pie(
    gender_counts,
    labels = paste0(labels, ": ", round(100 * gender_counts / sum(gender_counts), 1), "%"),
    main = paste("Gender Distribution at", university$`HE provider`),
    col = c("#FF9999", "#66B3FF", "#99FF99")  # Add colors for better visualization
  )
}

Combined Gender Distribution for Newcastle

# Combine Newcastle universities
combined_newcastle <- newcastle_data %>% 
  summarise(Female = sum(Female), Male = sum(Male), Unknown = sum(Unknown))

gender_counts <- c(combined_newcastle$Female, combined_newcastle$Male, combined_newcastle$Unknown)
labels <- c("Female", "Male", "Unknown")

# Plot pie chart
pie(gender_counts, 
    labels = paste0(labels, ": ", round(100 * gender_counts / sum(gender_counts), 1), "%"), 
    main = "Overall Gender Distribution in Newcastle")

# Load libraries
library(readxl)
library(ggplot2)

# Read the Excel file and load Middlesbrough data sheets directly
middle_22_23 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "Middle22-23")
middle_21_22 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "Middle21-22")
middle_20_21 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "Middle20-21")

# Combine the gender data from all three sheets
gender_data <- rbind(
  middle_22_23[, c("Female", "Male", "Unknown")],
  middle_21_22[, c("Female", "Male", "Unknown")],
  middle_20_21[, c("Female", "Male", "Unknown")]
)

# Summarize the total counts for each gender
gender_totals <- colSums(gender_data, na.rm = TRUE)

# Convert to a data frame for plotting
gender_df <- data.frame(
  Gender = names(gender_totals),
  Count = as.numeric(gender_totals)
)

# Add percentage labels
gender_df$Percentage <- (gender_df$Count / sum(gender_df$Count)) * 100

# Create the pie chart with percentages
ggplot(gender_df, aes(x = "", y = Count, fill = Gender)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 1, color = "white") +
  coord_polar("y") +
  labs(title = "Overall Gender Distribution in Middlesbrough",
       x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        panel.grid = element_blank()) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(Percentage, 1), "%")),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Female" = "pink", "Male" = "blue", "Unknown" = "grey")) # Customize colors

# Install necessary libraries if not already installed
if (!require("readxl")) install.packages("readxl")
if (!require("ggplot2")) install.packages("ggplot2")

# Load libraries
library(readxl)
library(ggplot2)

# Read the Liverpool sheets
liver_22_23 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "Liver22-23")
liver_21_22 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "Liver21-22")
liver_20_21 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "Liver20-21")

# Standardize column names to lowercase for consistency
colnames(liver_22_23) <- tolower(colnames(liver_22_23))
colnames(liver_21_22) <- tolower(colnames(liver_21_22))
colnames(liver_20_21) <- tolower(colnames(liver_20_21))

# Extract relevant columns (female, male, and unknown) from all sheets
gender_data <- rbind(
  liver_22_23[, c("female", "male", "unknown")],
  liver_21_22[, c("female", "male", "unknown")],
  liver_20_21[, c("female", "male", "unknown")]
)

# Summarize the total counts for each gender
gender_totals <- colSums(gender_data, na.rm = TRUE)

# Convert to a data frame for plotting
gender_df <- data.frame(
  Gender = names(gender_totals),
  Count = as.numeric(gender_totals)
)

# Add percentage labels
gender_df$Percentage <- (gender_df$Count / sum(gender_df$Count)) * 100

# Create the pie chart
ggplot(gender_df, aes(x = "", y = Count, fill = Gender)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 1, color = "white") +
  coord_polar("y") +
  labs(title = "Overall Gender Distribution in Liverpool",
       x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        panel.grid = element_blank()) +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(Percentage > 0, paste0(round(Percentage, 1), "%"), "")), 
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("female" = "pink", "male" = "blue", "unknown" = "grey")) # Customize colors

Bar Chart for Gender Distribution in Newcastle, Middlesbrough, and Liverpool

# Load necessary libraries
library(readxl)
library(ggplot2)

# Load data for each city
newcastle_data <- read_excel("gender.xlsx", sheet = "Newcastle")
middlesbrough_data <- read_excel("gender.xlsx", sheet = "Middlebrough")
liverpool_data <- read_excel("gender.xlsx", sheet = "Liverpool")

# Set appropriate column names (adjust if needed)
colnames(newcastle_data) <- c("University", "Female", "Male", "Unknown", "Total")
colnames(middlesbrough_data) <- c("University", "Female", "Male", "Unknown", "Total")
colnames(liverpool_data) <- c("University", "Female", "Male", "Unknown", "Total")

# Aggregate gender data for each city
combined_newcastle <- colSums(newcastle_data[, c("Female", "Male", "Unknown")], na.rm = TRUE)
combined_middlesbrough <- colSums(middlesbrough_data[, c("Female", "Male", "Unknown")], na.rm = TRUE)
combined_liverpool <- colSums(liverpool_data[, c("Female", "Male", "Unknown")], na.rm = TRUE)

# Combine all cities into one data frame
locations <- c("Newcastle", "Middlesbrough", "Liverpool")
gender_totals <- data.frame(
  Location = rep(locations, each = 3),
  Gender = rep(c("Female", "Male", "Unknown"), times = 3),
  Count = c(combined_newcastle, combined_middlesbrough, combined_liverpool)
)

# Plot bar chart
ggplot(gender_totals, aes(x = Location, y = Count, fill = Gender)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge") +
  labs(title = "Gender Distribution Comparison Across Cities", x = "City", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2")

# Load necessary libraries
library(readxl)
library(reshape2)
## 
## Attaching package: 'reshape2'
## The following object is masked from 'package:tidyr':
## 
##     smiths
library(ggplot2)

# Load data for Newcastle across three years
newcastle_22_23 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "Newcastle 22-23")
newcastle_21_22 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "New21-22")
newcastle_20_21 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "new 20-21")

# Ensure column names are consistent
colnames(newcastle_22_23) <- c("University", "Female", "Male", "Unknown", "Total")
colnames(newcastle_21_22) <- c("University", "Female", "Male", "Unknown", "Total")
colnames(newcastle_20_21) <- c("University", "Female", "Male", "Unknown", "Total")

# Aggregate gender data for Newcastle
gender_trend <- data.frame(
  Year = c(2023, 2022, 2021),
  Female = c(sum(newcastle_22_23$Female, na.rm = TRUE),
             sum(newcastle_21_22$Female, na.rm = TRUE),
             sum(newcastle_20_21$Female, na.rm = TRUE)),
  Male = c(sum(newcastle_22_23$Male, na.rm = TRUE),
           sum(newcastle_21_22$Male, na.rm = TRUE),
           sum(newcastle_20_21$Male, na.rm = TRUE)),
  Unknown = c(sum(newcastle_22_23$Unknown, na.rm = TRUE),
              sum(newcastle_21_22$Unknown, na.rm = TRUE),
              sum(newcastle_20_21$Unknown, na.rm = TRUE))
)

# Melt the data for plotting
gender_trend_melt <- melt(gender_trend, id.vars = "Year", variable.name = "Gender", value.name = "Count")

# Line chart for gender trends
ggplot(gender_trend_melt, aes(x = Year, y = Count, color = Gender)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +  # Replaced size with linewidth
  labs(title = "Gender Trend in Newcastle (3 Academic Years)", x = "Year", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2")

# Load necessary libraries
library(readxl)
library(reshape2)
library(ggplot2)

# Load data for each year and location
newcastle_22_23 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "Newcastle 22-23")
newcastle_21_22 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "New21-22")
newcastle_20_21 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "new 20-21")

middlesbrough_22_23 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "Middle22-23")
middlesbrough_21_22 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "Middle21-22")
middlesbrough_20_21 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "Middle20-21")

liverpool_22_23 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "Liver22-23")
liverpool_21_22 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "Liver21-22")
liverpool_20_21 <- read_excel("All Gender.xlsx", sheet = "Liver20-21")

# Ensure column names are consistent
colnames(newcastle_22_23) <- colnames(newcastle_21_22) <- colnames(newcastle_20_21) <- c("University", "Female", "Male", "Unknown", "Total")
colnames(middlesbrough_22_23) <- colnames(middlesbrough_21_22) <- colnames(middlesbrough_20_21) <- c("University", "Female", "Male", "Unknown", "Total")
colnames(liverpool_22_23) <- colnames(liverpool_21_22) <- colnames(liverpool_20_21) <- c("University", "Female", "Male", "Unknown", "Total")

# Aggregate gender data for all years and locations
area_data <- data.frame(
  Year = rep(c(2023, 2022, 2021), times = 3),
  Location = rep(c("Newcastle", "Middlesbrough", "Liverpool"), each = 3),
  Female = c(
    sum(newcastle_22_23$Female, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(newcastle_21_22$Female, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(newcastle_20_21$Female, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(middlesbrough_22_23$Female, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(middlesbrough_21_22$Female, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(middlesbrough_20_21$Female, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(liverpool_22_23$Female, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(liverpool_21_22$Female, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(liverpool_20_21$Female, na.rm = TRUE)
  ),
  Male = c(
    sum(newcastle_22_23$Male, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(newcastle_21_22$Male, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(newcastle_20_21$Male, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(middlesbrough_22_23$Male, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(middlesbrough_21_22$Male, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(middlesbrough_20_21$Male, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(liverpool_22_23$Male, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(liverpool_21_22$Male, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(liverpool_20_21$Male, na.rm = TRUE)
  ),
  Unknown = c(
    sum(newcastle_22_23$Unknown, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(newcastle_21_22$Unknown, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(newcastle_20_21$Unknown, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(middlesbrough_22_23$Unknown, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(middlesbrough_21_22$Unknown, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(middlesbrough_20_21$Unknown, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(liverpool_22_23$Unknown, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(liverpool_21_22$Unknown, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(liverpool_20_21$Unknown, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
)

# Reshape the data for plotting
area_data_melt <- melt(area_data, id.vars = c("Year", "Location"), variable.name = "Gender", value.name = "Count")

# Area chart
ggplot(area_data_melt, aes(x = Year, y = Count, fill = Gender)) +
  geom_area(position = "stack", alpha = 0.6) +
  facet_wrap(~ Location, scales = "free") +
  labs(title = "Gender Trends Across Locations (Newcastle, Middlesbrough, Liverpool)", x = "Year", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2")

#########
# Load necessary libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

# Assuming 'dataset' is already loaded
# Reshape the data into long format and exclude 'Total' row
long_data <- dataset %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -c(`HE provider`, Total),   # Exclude 'HE provider' and 'Total' columns
    names_to = "Subject",              # Column names will be subjects
    values_to = "Count"                # Values will be the counts
  ) %>%
  filter(`HE provider` != "Total")     # Exclude the row where 'HE provider' is "Total"

# Loop through each HE provider to create separate pie charts
unique_providers <- unique(long_data$`HE provider`)  # Get unique universities

# Generate pie charts for each provider
for (provider in unique_providers) {
  # Filter data for the current provider
  provider_data <- long_data %>%
    filter(`HE provider` == provider)
  
  # Calculate percentages for each Subject
  provider_data <- provider_data %>%
    mutate(Percentage = (Count / sum(Count)) * 100)
  
  # Add a label column for displaying Subject and Percentage
  provider_data <- provider_data %>%
    mutate(Label = paste0(round(Percentage, 1), "%"))  # Only display the percentage
  
  # Create a pie chart for the current provider
  pie_chart <- ggplot(provider_data, aes(x = "", y = Count, fill = Subject)) +
    geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 1) +      # Full bar to create the pie
    coord_polar("y", start = 0) +                 # Convert bar chart to pie chart
    geom_text(aes(label = Label),                 # Add percentage labels
              position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), # Center the labels in the slice
              size = 2,                           # Adjust font size
              color = "black") +                  # Make text white for better visibility
    theme_void() +                                # Remove axes and background
    labs(
      title = paste("Subject Distribution in", provider)
    ) +
    scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3")           # Use a visually appealing color palette
  
  # Print the pie chart
  print(pie_chart)
}

# Load necessary libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

# Assuming 'dataset' is already loaded
# Reshape the data into long format and exclude 'Total' row
long_data <- dataset_nonscience %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -c(`HE provider`, Total),   # Exclude 'HE provider' and 'Total' columns
    names_to = "Subject",              # Column names will be subjects
    values_to = "Count"                # Values will be the counts
  ) %>%
  filter(`HE provider` != "Total")     # Exclude the row where 'HE provider' is "Total"

# Loop through each HE provider to create separate pie charts
unique_providers <- unique(long_data$`HE provider`)  # Get unique universities

# Generate pie charts for each provider
for (provider in unique_providers) {
  # Filter data for the current provider
  provider_data <- long_data %>%
    filter(`HE provider` == provider)
  
  # Calculate percentages for each Subject
  provider_data <- provider_data %>%
    mutate(Percentage = (Count / sum(Count)) * 100)
  
  # Add a label column for displaying Subject and Percentage
  provider_data <- provider_data %>%
    mutate(Label = paste0(round(Percentage, 1), "%"))  # Only display the percentage
  
  # Create a pie chart for the current provider
  pie_chart <- ggplot(provider_data, aes(x = "", y = Count, fill = Subject)) +
    geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 1) +      # Full bar to create the pie
    coord_polar("y", start = 0) +                 # Convert bar chart to pie chart
    geom_text(aes(label = Label),                 # Add percentage labels
              position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), # Center the labels in the slice
              size = 2,                           # Adjust font size
              color = "black") +                  # Make text white for better visibility
    theme_void() +                                # Remove axes and background
    labs(
      title = paste("Non-Science Subject Distribution in", provider)
    ) +
    scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3")           # Use a visually appealing color palette
  
  # Print the pie chart
  print(pie_chart)
}

# Load necessary library

library(readxl)

# Read the data from the files in the working directory
dataset_22 <- read_excel("subject_of_study_2(science) 5.xlsx", sheet = 'newcastle(21-22)')
dataset_21 <- read_excel("subject_of_study_2(science) 5.xlsx", sheet = 'newcastle(20-21)')
dataset_nonscience_22 <- read_excel("subject_of_study(nonscience) 5.xlsx", sheet = 'newcastle(21-22)')
dataset_nonscience_21 <- read_excel("subject_of_study(nonscience) 5.xlsx", sheet = 'newcastle(20-21)')

# View the first few rows of each dataset to ensure proper loading

# Load the readxl library
library(readxl)

# Read the Newcastle sheet for science and nonscience datasets
total_science <- read_excel("subject_of_study_2(science).xlsx", sheet = "Newcastle")
total_nonscience <- read_excel("subject_of_study(nonscience).xlsx", sheet = "Newcastle")

# Extract the Total column
total_science_values <- total_science$Total
total_nonscience_values <- total_nonscience$Total

# Display the extracted totals
print(total_science_values)
## [1] 15860 15405     0 31265
print(total_nonscience_values)
## [1] 11985 17160   155 29300
science_long <- total_science %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -`HE provider`,
    names_to = "Subject",
    values_to = "Count"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::filter(`HE provider` != "Total") %>%
  mutate(Category = "Science")

non_science_long <- total_nonscience %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -`HE provider`,
    names_to = "Subject",
    values_to = "Count"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::filter(`HE provider` != "Total") %>%
  mutate(Category = "Non-Science")

combined_data <- bind_rows(science_long, non_science_long)

totals <- combined_data %>%
  group_by(`HE provider`, Category) %>%
  summarise(Total_Count = sum(Count), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(totals, aes(x = `HE provider`, y = Total_Count, fill = Category)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Total Number of Science vs Non-Science Subjects in Newcastle Universities",
    x = "University",
    y = "Total Count"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Science" = "skyblue", "Non-Science" = "orange")) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

# Load necessary library
library(ggplot2)
data <- read_excel("subject study (1).xlsx")

# Sample data preparation (Replace this with actual data loading in R)
data <- data.frame(
  Year = c("20/21", "21/22", "22/23", "20/21", "21/22", "22/23"),
  University = c("Newcastle", "Newcastle", "Newcastle", "Liverpool", "Liverpool", "Liverpool"),
  Subject = c("science", "science", "science", "nonscience", "nonscience", "nonscience"),
  Enrollment = c(1200, 1300, 1400, 800, 850, 900)
)

# Plot the data
ggplot(data, aes(x = Year, y = Enrollment, color = Subject, group = interaction(University, Subject))) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(~ University, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Trends in Enrollment for Science and Non-Science Subjects",
    x = "Academic Year",
    y = "Number of Students Enrolled",
    color = "Subject"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
## Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
## ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.
## This warning is displayed once every 8 hours.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.

# Load necessary library
library(ggplot2)

# Sample data preparation (Replace this with actual data loading in R)
data <- data.frame(
  City = c("Newcastle", "Newcastle", "Liverpool", "Liverpool", "Middlesbrough", "Middlesbrough"),
  Subject = c("science", "nonscience", "science", "nonscience", "science", "nonscience"),
  Enrollment = c(3500, 4200, 2800, 3000, 1500, 2000)
)

# Create a bar chart
ggplot(data, aes(x = City, y = Enrollment, fill = Subject)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge", color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Comparison of Science and Non-Science Enrollments",
    x = "City",
    y = "Number of Students Enrolled",
    fill = "Subject"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "top"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("science" = "blue", "nonscience" = "orange"))

# Load necessary library
library(ggplot2)

# Sample data preparation (Replace this with actual data loading in R)
data <- data.frame(
  Year = c("20/21", "21/22", "22/23", "20/21", "21/22", "22/23"),
  Subject = c("science", "science", "science", "nonscience", "nonscience", "nonscience"),
  Enrollment = c(1500, 1800, 2000, 3200, 3500, 4000)
)

# Filter for Newcastle data (if needed in a larger dataset)
# data <- subset(data, City == "Newcastle")

# Create an area chart
ggplot(data, aes(x = Year, y = Enrollment, fill = Subject, group = Subject)) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.6, color = "black", size = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Trend of Science and Non-Science Enrollments in Newcastle",
    x = "Academic Year",
    y = "Number of Students Enrolled",
    fill = "Subject"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "top"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("science" = "blue", "nonscience" = "orange"))

# Sample data preparation (Replace this with actual data loading in R)

  Year = c("20/21", "21/22", "22/23", "20/21", "21/22", "22/23"),
  Subject = c("science", "science", "science", "nonscience", "nonscience", "nonscience"),
  Enrollment = c(1200, 1400, 1600, 2500, 2700, 3000)
)

# Filter for Liverpool data (if needed in a larger dataset)
# data <- subset(data, City == "Liverpool")

# Create an area chart
ggplot(data, aes(x = Year, y = Enrollment, fill = Subject, group = Subject)) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.6, color = "black", size = 0.5) +

    title = "Trend of Science and Non-Science Enrollments in Liverpool",
    x = "Academic Year",
    y = "Number of Students Enrolled",
    fill = "Subject"

    legend.position = "top"
  ) +

# Load necessary library
library(ggplot2)

# Sample data preparation (Replace this with actual data loading in R)
data <- data.frame(
  Year = c("20/21", "21/22", "22/23", "20/21", "21/22", "22/23"),
  Subject = c("science", "science", "science", "nonscience", "nonscience", "nonscience"),
  Enrollment = c(800, 900, 1000, 2000, 2200, 2500)
)

# Filter for Middlesbrough data (if needed in a larger dataset)
# data <- subset(data, City == "Middlesbrough")

# Create an area chart
ggplot(data, aes(x = Year, y = Enrollment, fill = Subject, group = Subject)) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.6, color = "black", size = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Trend of Science and Non-Science Enrollments in Middlesbrough",
    x = "Academic Year",
    y = "Number of Students Enrolled",
    fill = "Subject"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "top"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("science" = "purple", "nonscience" = "yellow"))

# Load necessary library
library(ggplot2)

# Sample data preparation (Replace this with actual data loading in R)
data <- data.frame(
  City = rep(c("Newcastle", "Liverpool", "Middlesbrough"), each = 6),
  Year = rep(c("20/21", "21/22", "22/23"), times = 6),
  Subject = rep(c("science", "nonscience"), times = 9),
  Enrollment = c(1500, 3200, 1800, 3500, 2000, 4000, 1200, 2500, 1400, 2700, 1600, 3000,
                 800, 2000, 900, 2200, 1000, 2500)
)

# Create a stacked bar chart
ggplot(data, aes(x = Year, y = Enrollment, fill = Subject)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "stack", color = "black") +
  facet_wrap(~ City, nrow = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Comparison of Science and Non-Science Enrollments Across Cities",
    x = "Academic Year",
    y = "Number of Students Enrolled",
    fill = "Subject"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("science" = "blue", "nonscience" = "orange")) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "top"
  )

# Load necessary library
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)

# Load the dataset from the working directory
data <- read_excel("gender_distribution_with_years(outside).xlsx", sheet = "gender_distribution_dataset_wit")

# Summarize data by gender
combined_newcastle <- data %>%
  group_by(Gender) %>%
  summarise(Count = n())

# Extract counts and labels for the pie chart
gender_counts <- combined_newcastle$Count
labels <- combined_newcastle$Gender

# Create a pie chart
pie(
  gender_counts,
  labels = paste0(labels, ": ", round(100 * gender_counts / sum(gender_counts), 1), "%"),
  main = "Overall Gender Distribution in Newcastle with outside data"
)

# Load necessary libraries
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

# Load the dataset
data <- read_excel("gender_distribution_with_years(outside).xlsx", 
                   sheet = "gender_distribution_dataset_wit")

# Clean and prepare the data
data <- data %>%
  mutate(
    Year = as.factor(Year),   # Ensure Year is treated as a factor
    Gender = trimws(Gender)   # Remove extra spaces from Gender values
  )

# Summarize data by Year and Gender
gender_trend <- data %>%
  group_by(Year, Gender) %>%
  summarise(Count = n(), .groups = "drop")

# Debugging step: Check the summarized data
print(head(gender_trend))
## # A tibble: 6 × 3
##   Year    Gender Count
##   <fct>   <chr>  <int>
## 1 2020-21 Female   171
## 2 2020-21 Male     163
## 3 2021-22 Female   179
## 4 2021-22 Male     154
## 5 2022-23 Female   180
## 6 2022-23 Male     153
# Plot the data
gender_plot <- ggplot(gender_trend, aes(x = Year, y = Count, color = Gender, group = Gender)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +  # Add lines for gender trends
  geom_point(size = 3) +        # Add points for clarity
  labs(
    title = "Gender Trend in Newcastle (3 Academic Years)",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10)
  )

# Print the plot explicitly (necessary in some R environments)
print(gender_plot)

# Optional: Save the plot to a file
ggsave("gender_trend_plot.png", plot = gender_plot, width = 8, height = 5)
